# term: IRI / blank node / literal (the three RDF term types)
meaning: Every node/value in RDF is one of three: an IRI (globally unique identifier naming a resource); a blank node (a resource that exists but has no IRI — local, existential "something"); a literal (a concrete data value = lexical form + datatype, optionally a language tag). Subjects are IRIs/blank nodes; predicates are IRIs; objects may be any of the three.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): IRI → a named thing; blank node → "some unnamed thing such that…"; literal → a fixed data value. "Book123 has author some person whose name is 'Jane'."

meaning: The class of everything describable in RDF — every IRI, blank node, and literal denotes a resource, so it is the top class (every class is a subclass of it, every individual an instance).
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): Resource → "thing"/"anything": "Each Person is a thing."

meaning: The class whose instances are themselves classes; "C rdf:type rdfs:Class" says C is a category of things. Corresponds to a noun concept / general type.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): An rdfs:Class is a general concept: "Person is a concept."

meaning: The class of all properties — the relations used as predicates; "p rdf:type rdf:Property" says p connects a subject to an object. Properties are first-class resources (can have domain/range/sub-properties/labels).
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): A relation / fact-type: "a person is the author of a book."

meaning: The property stating an individual is an instance of a class; "x rdf:type C" asserts x belongs to C. Its rdfs:range is rdfs:Class (its rdfs:domain is rdfs:Resource). Abbreviated "a" in Turtle.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): is a/"is an instance of": "Alice rdf:type Person" → "Alice is a Person."

meaning: One class is a subclass of another — every instance of the subclass is necessarily an instance of the superclass; transitive and reflexive under RDFS entailment.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): Each Student is a Person. Drives the is-a hierarchy.

meaning: One property is a subproperty of another — whenever the subproperty relates x to y, the superproperty does too; transitive and reflexive under RDFS entailment.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): If a person has a mother, then that person has a parent (hasMother is a kind of hasParent).

meaning: The class of the subjects of a property: if p has domain C, anything that is the subject of a p-triple is inferred to be in C. Licenses inference, not validation; multiple domains intersect.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): Only a Person can be the author of something.

meaning: The class of the objects/values of a property: if p has range C, anything that is the object of a p-triple is inferred to be in C (a datatype for data-valued properties). Inferential, not a constraint; multiple ranges intersect.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): Whatever someone is the author of is a Book.

meaning: A plain, human-readable name for a resource — no logical/inferential weight, purely presentational; often language-tagged.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): the display name: a term whose IRI is ex:authorOf with label "is the author of" verbalizes using that label.

meaning: A human-readable description/note/definition of a resource — non-inferential documentation, frequently language-tagged.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): the definition/description text: the comment on a class becomes the glossary entry's explanatory sentence.

meaning: Indicates a resource providing additional information about the subject — links to related material without claiming it defines the subject (a navigational hint).
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): for more information, see: "Person — see also: the FOAF Person page."

meaning: A subproperty of rdfs:seeAlso naming the resource (typically an ontology/namespace) that defines the subject — names the authoritative source.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): is defined by: "The term authorOf is defined by the Publishing Ontology."

meaning: The class of all literal values (strings, numbers, dates); a subclass of rdfs:Resource and the superclass of all datatypes — groups "data value" resources as opposed to entities.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): a data value: a property whose range is rdfs:Literal → "has a value that is some data value (text/number/etc.)."

meaning: The class whose instances are datatypes (xsd:string, xsd:integer, xsd:date) — each defines a lexical space, value space, and mapping; each is an instance of rdfs:Datatype and a subclass of rdfs:Literal.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): the value type: "birthDate has range xsd:date" → "the birth date is a calendar date."

meaning: The datatype of language-tagged string literals (RDF 1.1) — value = a string + a non-empty language tag, e.g. "colour"@en-GB; such literals must not also carry an explicit datatype IRI. (RDF 1.2 adds optional base direction.)
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): text in a specific language: "Personne"@fr → "the French name is Personne."

meaning: The class of reified statements — a resource standing for a triple so you can make statements about it; reconstructed via subject/predicate/object. Asserting a reification does not assert the original triple (used for provenance/beliefs/annotations). (RDF 1.2 triple terms are the modern successor.)
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): the claim/fact that…: "The statement that Alice authored Book123 was asserted by Bob on 2020-01-01."

meaning: The three RDF Container classes: Bag (unordered, duplicates allowed), Seq (ordered sequence, order significant), Alt (alternatives, first is default). Members attach via rdf:_1, rdf:_2, … (instances of rdfs:ContainerMembershipProperty, subproperties of rdfs:member). Containers are "open."
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): Bag → "a group of (in no particular order)"; Seq → "an ordered list of"; Alt → "one of the following alternatives."

meaning: A finite, closed ordered collection as a linked list: each node has rdf:first (its element) and rdf:rest (the remainder), terminating with rdf:nil (the empty list). Unlike containers, a well-formed list is closed. Backs Turtle/SPARQL "( … )" syntax.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): the ordered list consisting of exactly…: "The author order is exactly: Alice, then Bob, then Carol (and no others)."

meaning: A general-purpose property for the main value of a structured resource that has extra qualifiers — no special semantics, a conventional idiom (core quantity via rdf:value, units/precision via other properties).
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): has principal value: a measurement with rdf:value 21 and unit "Celsius" → "the temperature is 21 degrees Celsius."

meaning: Two normatively defined RDF datatypes for markup-content literals whose support is optional for implementations. rdf:HTML (new in RDF 1.1) types an HTML-fragment value; rdf:XMLLiteral (carried from RDF 1.0, redefined in 1.1) types an XML-fragment value. Equality is by parsed value, not raw string.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): rich-text/markup content: "the description is the HTML fragment bold claim."

meaning: A super-property of all container membership properties (rdf:_1, rdf:_2, …); "C rdfs:member x" means x is a member of container C — lets readers treat membership uniformly regardless of position.
sbvrReading (TSV; '#' line = comment): is a member of: "Alice is a member of the committee," abstracting over the position index.
